title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: Prices Rise Ahead of Q1 2025 as Passed Regulatory Amendments Take Effect
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights:

(9th December â€“ 15th December)
  • A total of 378,298 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a fall of 28.90% week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades decreased by 42.90% WoW as 879,298 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price increased this week to $76.67/MTÂ for Type 1Â transfers, recording a 0.07%Â rise WoW; All Non-Zero transfers rose by 8.85% in price, reaching $72.83/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price decreased by 2.14% WoW; On 17th Dec, the spot price stood at $75.38/MT. The benchmark price stood at $75.69/MT on 17th
  • D3 RIN prices decreased by 10.53% WoW and D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices increase by 4.84% WoW.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

The market is showing signs of strategic positioning ahead of anticipated regulatory changes, with prices reflecting expectations of a constrained credit supply starting Q1 2025. The slight rise in CA LCFS Type 1 credit prices and increased prices for non-zero transfers suggest market participants are bracing for stricter CI benchmarks, accelerated ZEV adoption targets, and expanded feedstock pathways. As these regulations take effect, credit generation is likely to face limitations, potentially driving prices upward. This tightening supply dynamic may spur further strategic trading and feedstock diversification as stakeholders adapt to the evolving regulatory framework.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_590661" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 prices recorded a 0.07% WoW rise, increasing from $76.62 to $76.67 this week, with all non-zero credit prices increased by 8.85% WoW, rising from $66.91 to $72.83. Type 1 trades saw 378,298 credits exchanged, reflecting a 28.90% WoW decrease. Similarly, the total transaction volume for all non-zero trades declined by 42.90% WoW to reach 879,298 credits.

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_590662" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices decreased marginally by 2.14% WoW, reaching $75.53/MT. On 17th December, the average spot price was $75.38/MT, while the benchmark, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2024, stood at $75.69/MT as of 17th December.

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_590663" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 59 trades were reported in the market, with ten high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $79.00 for two Type 2 transactions with a volume of 10,000 credits. Last week, the lowest transfer by price was $61.00 per credit of Type 2, reported for a transaction at a volume of 5,000 credits. The smallest volume transfers of 2,000 credits of Type 2 category, were undertaken for price of $76.50 whereas the largest volume of transfer was reported at 75,000 MT of Type 2 category at $64.50.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_590664" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] Trading volumes in November 2024 experienced a significant decline, despite rising prices. The month saw a total volume of only 2.01 MMT, a steep drop from October's record-high 6.4 MMT. Average Type 1 prices for November rose to $73.07/MT, up from $65.93/MT in October. In the first half of December, this upward trend in prices continued, with the Average Type 1 price climbing to $76.64/MT. During the same period, trading volumes reached 2.42 MMT, surpassing the total volume recorded in November.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_590665" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 9th Dec â€“ 15th Dec, total VPT recorded a 30.32% increase (WoW), with all non-zero prices rising over the previous week.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN weekly price trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices decreased by 10.53% WoW and D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices increase by 4.84% WoW. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming dates

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this week

Aemetis Biogas Starts Production from Tenth Dairy Digester with Additional Digesters in Final Stages of Commissioning and Construction. Aemetis, Inc. (NASDAQ: AMTX), a renewable natural gas and renewable fuels company focused on low and negative carbon intensity products, announced that its Aemetis Biogas subsidiary started producing renewable natural gas (RNG) from its tenth anaerobic digester built to process waste from a dairy in Stanislaus County, California.Â  The newly constructed digesters for five additional dairies are in final stages of commissioning and construction, which is scheduled to expand the total Aemetis Biogas operations to twelve digesters processing waste from sixteen dairies.Â  Combined, the digesters are designed to produce an estimated 550,000 MMBtu per year of RNG in year 2025, an 80% increase from the current production capacity of 300,000 MMBtu per year. LF Bioenergy Expands Renewable Natural Gas Project Footprint, Bringing its Cumulative Environmental Benefits Equivalent to Reducing 60 Million Gasoline Miles. Â LF Bioenergy,Â a renewable natural gas (RNG) company that partners with farmers to produce energy from methane capture, announced the expansion of its RNG project footprint with four operating facilities and two under construction across the U.S. The operational RNG projects result in a cumulative environmental benefit equal to reducing 60 million gasoline miles. California Approves $1.4 billion Plan to Build Thousands More Vehicle Chargers, Boost ZEV Infrastructure. The California Energy Commission (CEC) approved a $1.4 billion investment plan that accelerates progress on the stateâ€™s EV charging and hydrogen refueling goals. These investments will help deploy infrastructure for light, medium, and heavy-duty zero-emission vehicles (ZEV) across California, expanding the most extensive charging and hydrogen refueling network in the country. Docket on Renewable Fuel Standard Program: Partial Waiver of 2024 Cellulosic Biofuel Standard Published in Federal Register. The docket on EPA's proposal for the waiver of the cellulosic biofuel volume obligations for 2024 has been published in the Federal Register. EPA is proposing to partially waive the 2024 cellulosic biofuel volume requirement and revise the associated percentage standard under the Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS) program due to a shortfall in cellulosic biofuel production. As a result of this proposed change, this action also proposes to extend the RFS compliance reporting deadline for the 2024 compliance year. This action also proposes several minor revisions related to the biogas provisions of the RFS program. Comments on the proposed rule must be received on or before January 21, 2025. EPA will hold a virtual public hearing on December 20, 2024. EPA Announces $8.4M for Clean Heavy-Duty Vehicles in Washington. The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) announced that the Washington Department of Ecology, Confederated Tribes of the Colville Reservation and Woodland Public Schools have been selected to receive a total of $8,396,706 in funding to assist in the purchase of 28 zero-emission vehicles through its first-ever Clean Heavy-Duty Vehicles Grant Program. The Washington Department of Ecology has been selected to receive an anticipated $3,887,706 to replace 13 diesel school buses with electric school buses. These buses are owned by four school districts, all of which experience high exposures to diesel pollution. Funding provided by EPA will be stacked on state grant funds appropriated to Ecologyâ€™s school bus program.
